stories.headline: Front Range passenger rail project wins pivotal RTD and state board votes, clearing path to 2029 launch
stories.summary:
Regional Transportation District directors and four state boards on Tuesday approved initial funding for a $332 million passenger rail project linking Denver, Boulder and Fort Collins using existing Burlington Northern Santa Fe Railway freight tracks. The "starter service," expected to launch by January 2029, would operate three 80-minute round-trips daily at speeds up to 79 miles per hour, stopping in eight cities including Westminster, Broomfield, Longmont, Louisville and Loveland.

RTD directors voted 14 to 1 to approve an initial funding commitment and pledged $156 million overall toward the project. The Colorado Transportation Investment Office committed $176 million plus $10 million to $12 million annually for operating costs, with the Colorado Clean Transit Enterprise board approving a matching annual operations contribution.

Design and engineering work is expected to be mostly completed by the end of 2026, with the first $5.8 million from RTD and $3.8 million from CTIO released immediately. A final agreement with BNSF must be executed by June 15 after receiving all five required board approvals.

The project is seen as a first step toward a broader Front Range Passenger Rail system eventually linking Fort Collins to Pueblo with 10 trains a day along the Interstate 25 corridor. Several transit advocates referenced RTD's failure to complete the FasTracks system — funded by sales taxes since 2005 — as context for why the new partnership model is significant.

stories.headline: Bipartisan bill would require governor's legislative liaisons to register and disclose stances like lobbyists
stories.summary:
Colorado lawmakers are advancing a bipartisan measure that would require legislative liaisons — staff employed by state agencies and the governor's office to influence legislation — to register and disclose their positions on bills the same way outside lobbyists are required to do. Senate Bill 146 passed the Senate 30 to 4 earlier this month and cleared the House State, Civic, Military, & Veterans Affairs Committee unanimously on Monday.

Gov. Jared Polis is pushing back against the proposal, with spokesperson Eric Maruyama calling it "absurd" and characterizing the bill as an attempt to limit the governor's office from meaningfully participating in the legislative process. Supporters of the bill, including both Democratic and Republican sponsors, argue the current system allows the executive branch to withhold its positions on legislation until the last minute, undermining good-faith negotiations.

"We've just experienced a unique set of years in Colorado history where we've seen an unprecedented level of involvement from the executive branch into the legislature," said Rep. Meg Froelich, an Englewood Democrat and bill co-sponsor. Republican Rep. Dusty Johnson of Fort Morgan said the measure simply seeks a "transparent" and "even playing field" between the branches.

Currently, lobbyists must register a stance on bills they seek to influence with the Secretary of State's Office, making that information publicly available. Legislative liaisons performing equivalent work face no such disclosure requirements.

stories.headline: Colorado legislature advances nuclear power revival bill as environmental groups vow opposition
stories.summary:
A Colorado House committee heard testimony Thursday on House Bill 1337, which would create a nuclear permitting coordinator in state government, allow utilities to charge customers up to $20 million for nuclear research expenses, and encourage cities to volunteer to host new nuclear power plants. Supporters, including bill co-sponsor Rep. Alex Valdez, D-Denver, and Xcel Energy, argue growing electricity demands and improved reactor designs make nuclear power a necessary part of Colorado's clean energy future.

Xcel Energy has been actively working toward nuclear development in Colorado and backed the bill's creation. Advocates note that Colorado's legislature officially added nuclear to the state's list of allowed clean energy sources in 2025, and that the technology produces fewer greenhouse gas emissions than natural gas plants being proposed as dispatchable backup power.

A coalition of environmental and economic justice nonprofits is opposing the measure, arguing that nuclear power poses unacceptable risks and that some clean-energy allies have abandoned the cause in favor of economic development. The groups say they will present a united front against the bill as it moves through the legislature.

Gov. Jared Polis has not indicated whether he would sign the bill in its current form, though his office said he supports nuclear energy "as part of Colorado's future" and is an enthusiast for efforts to create "reliable, safe and affordable clean energy."

stories.headline: Gov. Polis signs bipartisan Safe Haven bill extending newborn surrender window from 72 hours to 30 days
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis signed HB26-1024 into law Monday, extending Colorado's Safe Haven surrender window for newborns from 72 hours to 30 days. Under the revised law, parents may anonymously surrender infants at fire stations, hospitals or community emergency centers without fear of legal repercussions during that expanded period.

The bill received bipartisan support, with two Republican sponsors — Rep. Rebecca Keltie and Sen. Lisa Frizell — and two Democratic sponsors, Rep. Gretchen Rydin and Sen. Janice Marchman. Before the change, Colorado had one of the most restrictive Safe Haven timeframes in the nation.

A second provision in the law requires the Colorado Department of Human Services to establish a pathway for reunification if parents change their minds after surrendering a newborn. Supporters cited evidence of heightened postpartum vulnerability, higher surrender rates in states with less restrictive timeframes, and CDC data showing infants face the greatest risk of homicide within their first month of life.

The legislative declaration accompanying the bill stated the changes were introduced to provide a "safer and more supportive process for parents in crisis."

stories.headline: Polis signs puppy mill ban and veterinary access bills into law
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis signed two animal welfare bills into law this week, including HB26-1011, which prohibits pet stores in Colorado from selling, leasing, bartering or otherwise transferring ownership of dogs or cats. The measure — nicknamed the "Pistol the Pomeranian Protection Act" by Majority Leader Monica Duran, who championed the effort for eight years — is intended to cut off the pet store pipeline that critics say drives demand for puppy mills.

The bill signing was accompanied by an adoption event at which Humane Colorado brought shelter puppies seeking homes. First Gentleman Marlon Reis said the law affirms that "Colorado is a place where compassion, dignity, and transparency matter."

Polis also signed HB26-1198, the Access to Veterinary Care bill, which allows veterinarians to prescribe certain medications in-house and establishes a drug donation program enabling unused prescribed pet medicine to be donated to veterinary practices or animal shelters. Sponsors said the measure is designed to expand access to veterinary services and reduce barriers for pet owners.

Both bills had bipartisan sponsorship and are among several animal-welfare measures signed during the current legislative session.

stories.headline: Denver police use of face masks during drug raid draws scrutiny under new city law
stories.summary:
A drug operation conducted by Denver Police in north Denver on April 16 has drawn criticism from advocates after officers were photographed wearing face masks during the raid, raising questions about compliance with a recently enacted city law. Critics say masked officers erode community trust and accountability, particularly in undercover or plainclothes operations where residents cannot identify who is law enforcement.

Denver Police argue that mask use should be permitted in certain undercover operations, citing officer safety and the integrity of ongoing investigations as justifications. The department has not publicly detailed the specific legal basis for mask use during the April 16 operation.

Advocates pushing back on the practice pointed residents toward the city's Health and Safety Committee — composed of Denver City Council members — as a channel for raising concerns about police conduct. The debate reflects broader tensions in Denver over police transparency and accountability policies enacted in recent years.

The incident has reignited discussion about how the city enforces its own public-safety laws when the subjects of those laws are city employees, and whether existing oversight mechanisms are sufficient.

stories.headline: Xcel Energy tells Denver City Council that public safety power shut-offs are a "last resort" amid wildfire risk
stories.summary:
Xcel Energy officials briefed the Denver City Council's Transportation and Infrastructure Committee on Wednesday about wildfire mitigation efforts and the utility's $1.2 billion, five-year investment in Denver's electric grid. Representatives said public safety power shut-offs — used twice in December 2025 west of Denver during extreme wind conditions — are considered only as a last resort when three simultaneous factors are present: extreme winds, relative humidity of 20% or lower, and low fuel moisture.

The December 2025 shut-offs, triggered by winds of up to 96 mph, affected more than 135,000 customers, including 12,000 in Jefferson and Boulder counties. Officials noted it was the first time the National Weather Service had issued a "particularly dangerous situation" red-flag warning for the Front Range, and that Denver itself came close to experiencing a shut-off but was spared.

Xcel's community resiliency manager, Lyle Moore, told the committee the utility has added more than 100 weather stations, drones and artificial intelligence tools to detect smoke and wildfires earlier and coordinate with fire and public safety agencies. "We're using AI to detect smoke and detect those wildfires quicker," Moore said.

Officials acknowledged that beyond extreme wind, factors including car crashes, severe weather, wildlife and overgrown vegetation can also trigger fires and potentially power shut-offs, underscoring the broad range of risks utilities must manage as drought conditions persist along the Front Range.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers pull two marijuana bills on THC drink limits and potency-based taxation
stories.summary:
A pair of cannabis-related bills died this week after their sponsors withdrew them ahead of committee votes. Senate Bill 164 would have raised the THC limit in hemp-derived beverages from 1.75 milligrams per serving to 10 milligrams per 12-ounce serving, potentially expanding their sale at bars, liquor stores and grocery stores. Sen. Julie Gonzales, a Denver Democrat and sponsor, cited a lack of "political will" as the reason for pulling the bill.

The second measure, Senate Bill 161, would have referred to voters a proposal to shift marijuana product testing to the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment and restructure the marijuana sales tax to be based on potency rather than volume. Sponsor Sen. Marc Snyder of Manitou Springs said he needed more time to gather feedback after joining the bill late following the resignation of its original sponsor in February.

Co-sponsor Sen. Kyle Mullica, a Thornton Democrat, had framed the potency-based tax as a tool to help keep high-potency marijuana products out of the hands of children. Supporters said the existing testing framework lacked sufficient rigor.

The withdrawals leave Colorado's marijuana regulatory framework unchanged heading into the final weeks of the legislative session, and advocates on both sides acknowledged the issues are likely to resurface in future sessions.

stories.headline: Opus completes Centennial industrial building, lands Tesla Collision Center as tenant
stories.summary:
Developer Opus has completed Catalyst Industrial, a new industrial building in the Centennial area of the Denver metro, and has signed Tesla as one of its first tenants. Tesla will operate a Collision Center at the facility, marking another expansion of the electric vehicle maker's service footprint in the region.

The completion of Catalyst Industrial reflects continued demand for modern industrial and commercial service space in Denver's southern suburbs, where available inventory has tightened in recent years. The Tesla Collision Center will provide repair and body work services for Tesla vehicles in the south metro area.

Opus is a national developer with an active portfolio in the Denver market. Specific financial terms of the Tesla lease were not disclosed.

stories.headline: Cool, wet weather to continue in metro Denver through Friday before weekend warm-up; heavy mountain snow expected
stories.summary:
Metro Denver is forecast to see continued cool, rainy conditions through Friday, with an 80% chance of rain Thursday and highs around 47 degrees. Overnight lows are expected to fall to 37 degrees Thursday night before a gradual warm-up brings partly sunny skies and highs in the upper 50s to low 70s over the weekend.

Mountains south of Interstate 70 are expected to receive the heaviest snowfall, with three to 10 inches forecast from Wednesday night through Friday as a significant spring storm system moves through the state. Scattered snow showers are expected Wednesday with widespread accumulation beginning late Wednesday night.

Thunderstorms are possible in the Denver metro Wednesday afternoon, with a 50% chance of precipitation during the day rising to 60% overnight. Friday will again bring a 50% chance of rain before conditions clear.

The National Weather Service in Boulder is tracking the system, which follows an already wet stretch across the Front Range. Sunny skies are expected to return Saturday with a high near 58, climbing to 73 on Sunday.

stories.headline: Avalanche await second-round opponent, counting on Nicolas Roy to sustain playoff spark
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche are awaiting their second-round playoff opponent — either the Dallas Stars or the Minnesota Wild — after sweeping the Los Angeles Kings in the first round. Veteran forward Nicolas Roy, acquired in a March 5 trade from the Toronto Maple Leafs for a conditional fifth-round pick and a conditional 2027 first-round selection, was a key contributor in the sweep, recording points in three of four games including an overtime winner in Game 2.

Roy posted a plus-five rating in the series while averaging nearly 13 minutes of ice time per game on the third line alongside captain Gabriel Landeskog and center Nazem Kadri, who was also acquired at the trade deadline. Head coach Jared Bednar called Roy's play "amazing" and said he may adjust line combinations based on the second-round matchup.

Roy, 29, is a natural center playing wing to accommodate Kadri's arrival. He brings Stanley Cup experience from the Vegas Golden Knights' 2023 championship run, in which he recorded 11 points in 22 playoff games. "You go from definitely not making the playoffs to obviously the first team in the league," Roy said of his situation following the trade.

Colorado's retooled third line — featuring three players with Stanley Cup rings — was credited as a key factor in winning the matchup against a younger Kings third line. Bednar said Roy's defensive reliability and ability to generate offense can translate against any opponent in the second round.

stories.headline: Denver Broncos active in NFL offseason as draft night results draw AFC West comparisons
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos are generating buzz in NFL circles following the conclusion of the 2026 NFL Draft, with the team appearing in top-three NFL power rankings and attracting discussion about how the franchise fared compared to other AFC West rivals. Fan and analyst commentary points to depth-focused drafting strategy as a positive indicator for the team's direction under its current front office.

Quarterback discussions continue to circulate around the roster, with backups Zach Stidham and Sam Ehlinger drawing attention as depth options behind the starter. Available NFL free agents remain on the market as teams round out their rosters heading into the offseason program period.

The Broncos' offseason moves are being closely watched by the fan base as the franchise looks to build on recent momentum and compete in one of the NFL's most competitive divisions.

weather.notable: About 10 degrees below the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I got lost in a corn maze with my family. My wife was upset. I said, 'This is exactly what the pioneers went through.'

topStory.headline: London police declare Golders Green stabbings a terrorist incident; suspect arrested
topStory.summary:
London Metropolitan Police have declared a terrorist incident after two Jewish men were stabbed Wednesday morning in the Golders Green neighborhood of north London, a district with a large Jewish population. The victims, identified locally as Shilome Rand, 34, and Moshe Shine, 76, were treated at the scene and transported to hospital, where both remained in stable condition. Police said the attack occurred at approximately 11:16 a.m. local time on Highfield Avenue, off Golders Green Road.

A 45-year-old British national born in Somalia was arrested on suspicion of attempted murder after officers used a stun gun to subdue him. The suspect also attempted to stab the responding officers, though none were injured. Metropolitan Police Commissioner Sir Mark Rowley noted that the unarmed officers feared the suspect might be carrying an explosive device when he refused to show his hands, and said the man has a documented history of serious violence and mental health issues. Police have also linked the suspect to an earlier altercation Tuesday at an address in Southwark, in which a property occupant suffered minor injuries.

British Prime Minister Keir Starmer condemned what he called an "antisemitic attack" that was "utterly appalling," stating that "attacks on our Jewish community are attacks on Britain." He promised additional government funding for community protection and said authorities would "take measures to deal with malign state actors." London Mayor Sadiq Khan also condemned the attack and announced high-visibility police patrols in the area. Metropolitan Police Assistant Commissioner Laurence Taylor said counterterrorism officers are working to determine whether the attack deliberately targeted the Jewish community.

The attack comes amid what officials and community leaders describe as a rising tide of antisemitic incidents across the United Kingdom. Chief Rabbi Ephraim Mirvis called on institutions and leaders to move beyond words of condemnation to "meaningful action." Commissioner Rowley warned that "some individuals are being encouraged, persuaded or paid to commit acts of violence on behalf of foreign organizations and hostile states" and called for broader public condemnation of recent attacks on British Jews. Police have said a search is underway at an address in southeast London as the investigation continues.
